hero.heading: CoreLab Analytics
hero.description: An interactive platform that converts raw research data into immediate visual insights — replacing static papers with a dynamic dashboard.

descriptor.label: CoreLab Analytics is
descriptor.body: A quantitative correlational study on how computer laboratory usage predicts academic performance change among BSIT students — paired with a data visualization tool built to translate the findings into accessible evidence.

beats.label: Context
beats.body: IT education is fundamentally practical. Lecture material alone is insufficient — students in BSIT programs require the computer laboratory to run code, configure networks, and develop applied technical skills. Local and international studies confirm that ICT integration and guided laboratory access are tied to better student outcomes.

beats.label: The Gap
beats.body: Existing research focuses on general, end-of-term results. What is critically underexamined is performance shift within a single academic term — specifically, whether laboratory engagement during the Preliminary period corresponds to measurable academic improvement by the Midterm period. This study addresses that gap at STI College Malolos.

sections.title: Study Variables

cards.title: Frequency
cards.definition: Weekly laboratory hours attended, including both scheduled class time and voluntary extra lab sessions.
cards.measurement: Self-reported numeric hours per week (ratio scale). Winsorized: one outlier capped at 10 hrs.

cards.title: Intensity
cards.definition: Composite Behavioral Engagement Score — derived from five Likert-scale items measuring active technical behaviors (coding, configuration, debugging), with passive and non-technical behaviors reverse-coded.
cards.measurement: Aggregated score across Q8.1–Q8.5; Q8.2 and Q8.4 are reverse-coded. Scale: 5–25.

cards.title: Performance Change Score
cards.definition: The measurable shift in academic performance between grading periods — computed as Midterm Grade minus Preliminary Grade.
cards.measurement: Numerical grade difference per respondent. Positive = improvement.

sections.context: 86 BSIT students at STI College Malolos across five core subjects: Computer Programming 1 and 2, Event-Driven Programming, Systems Administration and Maintenance, and Introduction to Computing.

sections.title: Research Questions
sections.central: Does computer laboratory usage — measured through Frequency (weekly laboratory hours) and Intensity (composite behavioral engagement score) — correspond to changes in academic performance between the Preliminary and Midterm grading periods in selected core BSIT subjects at STI College Malolos?
subs: What is the relationship between laboratory usage and Preliminary academic performance?
What is the relationship between laboratory usage and Midterm academic performance?
Is increased laboratory usage associated with positive performance change between the Preliminary and Midterm grading periods?

hypotheses.label: H₁ (Alternative)
hypotheses.text: Higher laboratory usage is associated with a positive change in academic performance between the Preliminary and Midterm grading periods.

hypotheses.label: H₀₁ (Null)
hypotheses.text: Laboratory usage is not associated with a significant change in academic performance between the Preliminary and Midterm grading periods.

sections.title: Significance of the Study
sections.generalObjective: To examine the relationship between computer laboratory usage and changes in academic performance across the Preliminary and Midterm grading periods in selected core BSIT subjects at STI College Malolos.

objectives.text: Measure the Frequency and Intensity of computer laboratory usage among BSIT students.
Determine the relationship between laboratory usage and Preliminary academic performance.
Determine the relationship between laboratory usage and Midterm academic performance.
Assess whether increased laboratory usage corresponds to positive performance change, using the computed Performance Change Score.
Develop a web-based tool for survey distribution, data visualization, and dissemination of findings.

cards.title: Students
cards.body: Understanding how laboratory time translates to measurable academic progress, enabling more intentional use of available hours within the term.

cards.title: Faculty and Instructors
cards.body: Insight into when and how laboratory activities contribute most to performance, informing the design and pacing of laboratory exercises across the term.

cards.title: Administrators and Policymakers
cards.body: Empirical basis for scheduling decisions, laboratory access equity policies, and resource allocation at the institutional level.

cards.title: Future Researchers
cards.body: A methodological foundation for examining intra-term performance shifts in technical education contexts, with identified variables and gaps for extension.

panels.heading: Synthesis of Literature
panels.body: Three converging themes emerge from the reviewed literature: structured technology use improves student focus and engagement; equitable access to ICT resources determines whether students can convert instruction into applicable skills; and sustained laboratory practice builds technical competency over time. Together, these studies confirm that laboratory-based learning supports academic outcomes — but none directly examines how Frequency and Intensity of usage correlate with intra-term performance change. This study addresses that gap.

panels.heading: Theme 1 — Structured Technology Use and Academic Performance
panels.body: Guided, purposeful use of technology in the laboratory improves student focus and performance outcomes. Limniou (2021) found that students using devices under structured academic intent outperformed those with unrestricted access. Canoy et al. (2023) demonstrated beneficial effects of supervised lab sessions while identifying practical barriers: inadequate equipment, scheduling conflicts, and insufficient instructor preparation. The consistent finding: structure is as important as access.
panels.gap: Limited evidence exists on how structured lab use affects short-term, intra-term academic performance specifically in BSIT programs.

panels.heading: Theme 2 — Access to ICT Resources and Learning Outcomes
panels.body: Access to ICT resources is a prerequisite for skill development. Mohamed (2025) found that availability significantly impacts performance and recommended extending ICT access beyond laboratories. Siega (2025) confirmed a positive relationship between computer technology use and academic outcomes. Escubido et al. (2025) found that effective ICT integration in instruction improves performance, while persistent resource limitations continue to constrain student progress.
panels.gap: These studies measure access as a binary condition. None examines duration and behavioral depth of usage as a predictor of performance change across grading periods.

panels.heading: Theme 3 — Digital Skills Development Through Laboratory Practice
panels.body: Repeated hands-on laboratory engagement is the mechanism through which BSIT students develop technical competency. Vahid et al. (2023) found that students who consistently engaged in coding activities achieved higher grades, with consistent engagement being the operative variable. Cadiz-Gabejan and Takenaka (2021) found that initial computer literacy amplifies the benefit students derive from lab work.
panels.gap: Neither study quantifies behavioral intensity or separates it from time-based frequency as a distinct predictor.

panels.heading: Theoretical Framework
panels.body: Three theories collectively explain how laboratory usage may influence academic performance. Cognitive Load Theory accounts for how structured access reduces mental inefficiency. Constructivist Learning Theory explains how active engagement builds knowledge. Experiential Learning Theory connects repeated practice to measurable skill development. Together, they predict that consistent and intentional laboratory usage — high in both Frequency and Intensity — creates the conditions for a positive Performance Change Score.

panels.heading: Cognitive Load Theory — Sweller (1988)
panels.body: Students have limited mental capacity at any given time. Laboratory environments that are well-managed and consistently accessible reduce extraneous cognitive load — the effort spent on logistical friction — and redirect that capacity toward germane load, where actual learning occurs. High Frequency creates a stable, low-friction environment. High Intensity ensures that mental effort within that environment is directed at technical skill-building rather than passive presence.

panels.heading: Constructivist Learning Theory — Piaget (1972) & Vygotsky (1978)
panels.body: Knowledge is constructed through active experience and social interaction. Piaget's concept of schema formation through trial and correction, and Vygotsky's Zone of Proximal Development, both find direct expression in the computer laboratory. Increased laboratory hours expand the opportunity for knowledge construction. High behavioral engagement — active coding, troubleshooting, and configuration — is the mechanism through which that construction occurs.

panels.heading: Experiential Learning Theory — Kolb (1984)
panels.body: Learning proceeds through a four-stage cycle: concrete experience → reflective observation → abstract conceptualization → active experimentation. The laboratory provides the site for concrete experience. Frequency determines how often the cycle is entered. Intensity determines how fully it is engaged. Repeated consistently across a term, this cycle produces the measurable improvement captured by the Performance Change Score.

sections.title: Scope and Delimitation
sections.lead: What the study covers — and the boundaries that keep the findings interpretable.
leftItems: BSIT students at STI College Malolos with paired Preliminary + Midterm grades
Selected core IT subjects requiring computer laboratory use
Laboratory usage measured as Frequency (weekly hours) and Intensity (composite behavioral score)
Performance Change Score computed as Midterm − Preliminary
rightItems: Objective attendance logs / registrar grade records (study uses self-report)
External factors: tutoring, home internet, study habits, attendance in non-lab classes

sections.title: Quantitative Correlational Design
sections.lead: The study examined naturally occurring data — no variables were manipulated. Correlation answered the relationship questions; regression tested whether usage metrics predict performance change.

blocks.heading: Why Correlational
blocks.body: The aim was to determine the nature, degree, and direction of the statistical relationship between laboratory usage and academic performance — not to establish cause.

blocks.heading: Pearson r
blocks.body: Used to test the strength and direction of association between Frequency / Intensity and grades at each grading period.

blocks.heading: Linear Regression
blocks.body: Used to test whether the usage metrics significantly predict the Performance Change Score between Preliminary and Midterm.

blocks.heading: Limitation
blocks.body: Correlational design cannot establish causation — only that the variables move together within the sample.

sections.title: Participants and Sampling
sections.lead: Population: 406 BSIT students (after excluding 4th-year). Slovin's formula at 90% confidence / 10% margin → minimum 81. Final N = 86 valid responses retained.
leftItems: Initial population: BSIT students at STI College Malolos
rightItems: Enrolled BSIT student at STI College Malolos
Enrolled in / completed selected core IT subjects with lab component
Able to report attended laboratory hours per week
Able to self-evaluate technical engagement using the survey indicators
Has complete paired Preliminary + Midterm grades for the subject

sections.title: Data Collection Plan
sections.lead: A digitally distributed structured survey with informed consent, anonymous response handling, and a verification step for self-reported grades.

blocks.heading: Approval & Instrument
blocks.body: Institutional approval secured first; structured survey reviewed for clarity before distribution. Items covered weekly lab hours, behavioral engagement, subject, and Prelim + Midterm grades.

blocks.heading: Distribution
blocks.body: Survey link distributed digitally — class group chats, text messages, and QR codes — to reach BSIT students efficiently and organize responses for analysis.

blocks.heading: Ethics
blocks.body: Informed consent before participation; voluntary with right to withdraw; anonymous; data analyzed in aggregate only; access restricted to the research team.

blocks.heading: Data Preparation
blocks.body: Performance Change Score = Midterm − Preliminary. Frequency: one outlier of 20 hrs winsorized to 10. Intensity: 5 Likert items, passive items reverse-coded, then summed into the Composite Behavioral Score.

sections.title: Data Analysis Plan
sections.lead: Analysis ran in Google Sheets with the XL Miner Analysis ToolPak. α = 0.05 throughout.

blocks.heading: Descriptive Statistics
blocks.body: Mean, SD, min, max for weekly lab hours, Composite Behavioral Score, Preliminary Grade, Midterm Grade, and Performance Change Score.

blocks.heading: Reliability — Cronbach's α
blocks.body: Computed across the 5 Likert items of the Intensity metric to confirm internal consistency before using the composite score in inferential analysis.

blocks.heading: Pearson r — 4 Pairings
blocks.body: Frequency × Prelim, Intensity × Prelim, Frequency × Midterm, Intensity × Midterm — separating the two usage dimensions across both grading periods.

blocks.heading: Linear Regression
blocks.body: Simple regression for each metric, plus multiple regression with both predictors, to test whether Frequency and / or Intensity significantly predict the Performance Change Score.

finale.title: Ready to explore the data?
finale.description: Step into the tool to take the survey, or jump directly to the visualization dashboard.
finale.footer: © 2025 CoreLab Analytics — ITMAWD 12A research group · STI College Malolos
